name: pool-route
description: PreToolUse(Bash) pool auto-router + SessionStart routing-log snapshot, implemented in hexa-lang (`_pool_route.hexa`, invoked via `hexa run`). When the `pool` CLI's roster (`~/.pool/pool.json`) has ≥1 enabled host and a Bash command matches the heavy classifier (make · cargo · npm · gradle · pytest · go build · swift build · docker build · nvidia-smi · train · hexa kick/drill/loop/cc · `find $HOME/core/anima` big-tree scan · …) or a root-needing command, the command is rewritten via the hook `updatedInput` field to run on a pool host over ssh (`ssh <host> 'cd <workdir> && <cmd>'`) — transparent dispatch, not a suggestion. Routing is EXPLICIT-classifier only (0.6.0): the 0.5.7 load-escalation gate — which promoted any non-trivial cmd to heavy when summed non-claude CPU exceeded 150% — was removed because on a multi-core Mac that threshold trips constantly and shipped local-bound git/hexa-run/smoke commands to Linux hosts where their paths don't exist. Local-bound guard (0.6.0): `git`/`gh` commands and any `/Users/`·`/home/` absolute-path literal always run local. Zero-macOS-offload (0.6.1): macOS hosts are NEVER auto-dispatch candidates (project.tape @D s12 / pool @D p3) — the Mac is the workstation, not a pool worker, so macOS-capable commands (swift build · xcodebuild) run local on it rather than route to a separate macOS box; an explicit `pool on <macos-host>` still works via the pool CLI. Heavy-classified commands MUST use pool resources: the hook iterates every eligible host through a preflight (workdir present + `hexa <subverb> --help` dispatchable for kick/drill/loop/cc), routes to the first that passes, and if ALL fail emits a `permissionDecision: deny` with a per-host failure breakdown — never silently falls back to local. `hexa kick`/`drill`/`loop`/`cc` get `HEXA_LANG=$HOME/core/hexa-lang`; other hexa commands get `HEXA_LANG=$PWD`. Each routing decision appends one JSON line to `~/.pool/route-log.jsonl` (capped at 100); SessionStart reads the last 5 back as additionalContext. OS-capability filter + round-robin load balance. NO opt-out by design.
